facts.text: Coco Chanel introduced the little black dress in 1926, and Vogue called it 'Chanel's Ford' — meaning it was as universal as the Model T car.
The Met Gala, fashion's biggest night, has been held on the first Monday in May since 2005, though the event dates back to 1948.
Nike's Air Jordan 1 was banned by the NBA in 1985 for violating the league's uniform policy, and Nike paid the $5,000 fine per game, turning it into a marketing goldmine.
Vogue magazine was first published in 1892 as a weekly society gazette in New York before becoming the fashion bible it is today.
The Hermès Birkin bag was inspired by a chance meeting between actress Jane Birkin and Hermès CEO Jean-Louis Dumas on a flight in 1984.
London Fashion Week was established in 1984 and is held biannually in February and September at venues across the city.
Naomi Campbell was the first Black model to appear on the cover of French Vogue in August 1988 and the first to grace the cover of Time magazine's fashion issue.
The trench coat was originally designed for British military officers during World War I by Burberry, using waterproof gabardine fabric Thomas Burberry had patented in 1879.
Virgil Abloh founded Off-White in 2012 and became the first African-American artistic director of Louis Vuitton's menswear collection in 2018.
Levi Strauss and Jacob Davis patented the first riveted blue jeans on May 20, 1873, originally designed as durable workwear for miners.
Anna Wintour became editor-in-chief of American Vogue in 1988 and is known for her signature bob haircut and dark sunglasses.
The fashion industry produces about 10% of global carbon emissions — more than international flights and maritime shipping combined.
Supreme was founded in 1994 as a small skate shop on Lafayette Street in New York City and became a billion-dollar streetwear empire.
Christian Dior's 'New Look' debuted on February 12, 1947, featuring cinched waists and full skirts that revolutionized post-war fashion.
Converse Chuck Taylor All Stars were first produced in 1917 and are the best-selling basketball shoe of all time with over 800 million pairs sold.
Kate Moss was discovered at age 14 at JFK Airport in New York in 1988 and went on to define the 'heroin chic' aesthetic of the 1990s.
The average American throws away about 81 pounds of clothing per year, contributing to roughly 11.3 million tons of textile waste in U.S. landfills annually.
Paris Fashion Week is the oldest of the Big Four fashion weeks, with roots going back to 1945, though designer showcases in Paris date to the 1800s.
Alexander McQueen's Spring 1999 show featured model Shalom Harlow being spray-painted by two robotic arms on a rotating platform — one of fashion's most iconic runway moments.
Ray-Ban Aviators were originally developed in 1936 for U.S. military pilots to protect their eyes at high altitudes.
Gucci was founded in Florence, Italy, in 1921 by Guccio Gucci, who was inspired by the elegant luggage he saw while working at the Savoy Hotel in London.
The global sneaker resale market was valued at over $10 billion in 2025, with platforms like StockX and GOAT leading the secondary market.
Project Runway premiered on Bravo in 2004 and has run for over 20 seasons, making it one of the longest-running fashion competition shows.
Stella McCartney has been a pioneer of sustainable luxury fashion since launching her label in 2001, never using leather or fur in her designs.
The 1920s flapper dress symbolized women's liberation — its dropped waist and shorter hemline allowed freedom of movement for dancing the Charleston.
Cindy Crawford's iconic Pepsi commercial debuted during the 1992 Super Bowl and became one of the most memorable ads in history.
Milan Fashion Week began in 1958 and is known as the epicenter of Italian luxury fashion, showcasing houses like Prada, Versace, and Armani.
The Birkin bag has appreciated in value by an average of 14% annually, outperforming both the S&P 500 and gold as an investment.
Vivienne Westwood is credited with bringing punk fashion into the mainstream in the 1970s through her boutique on London's King's Road.
The white T-shirt became a fashion staple after Marlon Brando wore one in A Streetcar Named Desire (1951) and James Dean in Rebel Without a Cause (1955).
New York Fashion Week was founded in 1943 by Eleanor Lambert as 'Press Week' to draw attention away from French fashion during World War II.
Gianni Versace was tragically murdered on the steps of his Miami Beach mansion on July 15, 1997, and his sister Donatella took over the fashion empire.
The corset dominated women's fashion for over 400 years, from the 16th century through the early 1900s, sometimes reducing waists to as small as 13 inches.
StockX, the 'stock market of things' for sneakers, processed over $1.8 billion in sales within its first few years of operation.
Harper's Bazaar, founded in 1867, is the oldest continuously published fashion magazine in the United States.
Ralph Lauren started his fashion empire in 1967 by designing wide neckties, which he sold to Bloomingdale's under the Polo label.
The 'Big Four' fashion weeks — New York, London, Milan, and Paris — collectively show over 400 collections each season.
Tyra Banks became the first African-American woman to appear on the covers of GQ and the Sports Illustrated Swimsuit Issue.
Fast fashion brands can take a design from the runway to store shelves in as little as two weeks, compared to the traditional six-month fashion cycle.
Yves Saint Laurent introduced the women's tuxedo suit, 'Le Smoking,' in 1966, challenging gender norms and redefining power dressing.
The Devil Wears Prada (2006) was inspired by Lauren Weisberger's experience working as Anna Wintour's assistant at Vogue.
Balenciaga was founded by Cristóbal Balenciaga in 1919 in San Sebastián, Spain, and he was referred to by Christian Dior as 'the master of us all.'
The Nike Swoosh logo was designed by graphic design student Carolyn Davidson in 1971 for just $35.
Gigi Hadid made her New York Fashion Week debut in 2014 walking for Desigual and has since walked for nearly every major fashion house.
Victorian fashion dictated that women wear up to 37 pounds of clothing, including multiple petticoats, corsets, and bustles.
The Louis Vuitton monogram canvas was created in 1896 by Georges Vuitton, son of founder Louis, as an anti-counterfeiting measure.
Coco Chanel was born Gabrielle Bonheur Chanel on August 19, 1883, in Saumur, France.
Calvin Klein's provocative 1980 jeans ad featuring a 15-year-old Brooke Shields with the line 'Nothing comes between me and my Calvins' was banned by some TV stations.
The sneaker collaboration between Travis Scott and Nike for the Air Jordan 1 'Cactus Jack' became one of the most sought-after releases in sneaker history.
80s power dressing was defined by broad-shouldered blazers, bold colors, and statement accessories, popularized by shows like Dynasty and designers like Thierry Mugler.
The Rolex Submariner, introduced in 1953, was the first wristwatch waterproof to 100 meters and became an icon of luxury and adventure.
Thrifting and secondhand fashion grew 25 times faster than the broader retail market between 2019 and 2024.
Prada was founded in 1913 by Mario Prada as a leather goods shop in Milan's Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II.
Cara Delevingne's thick eyebrows challenged the thin-brow beauty standard and helped launch the 'bold brow' trend in the 2010s.
The Met Gala's 2019 theme was 'Camp: Notes on Fashion,' inspired by Susan Sontag's 1964 essay, and featured some of the most extravagant looks in the event's history.
Versace's iconic Medusa logo was chosen by Gianni Versace because Medusa made people fall in love with her, and there was no turning back.
The 1990s grunge fashion movement, led by bands like Nirvana and Pearl Jam, made flannel shirts, ripped jeans, and combat boots runway-worthy.
Tom Ford revived Gucci in the mid-1990s, transforming it from a near-bankrupt brand into a $4.3 billion powerhouse.
The Louis Vuitton Speedy bag, originally created in 1930, was made smaller at the request of Audrey Hepburn, resulting in the now-classic Speedy 25.
GQ magazine was founded in 1931 as Apparel Arts, a trade publication, before being renamed Gentlemen's Quarterly in 1958.
Alexander McQueen's 'Skull Scarf,' introduced around 2003, became one of the most copied accessories in fashion history.
Adidas Yeezy sneakers, designed in collaboration with Kanye West, generated over $1.7 billion in revenue for Adidas in 2022 before the partnership ended.
The 1960s mod fashion movement, centered in London, was characterized by miniskirts, geometric prints, and go-go boots, championed by designer Mary Quant.
Christian Louboutin's signature red-soled shoes were inspired when he painted red nail polish on a shoe prototype in 1993.
The Kelly bag by Hermès was renamed after Grace Kelly was photographed using it to shield her pregnant belly from paparazzi in 1956.
Fashion week 'front row' culture became a celebrity spectacle in the early 2000s, with brands paying stars up to $100,000 to attend shows.
Heidi Klum hosted Project Runway from its premiere in 2004 through Season 16 in 2017, coining the catchphrase 'In fashion, one day you're in, and the next day you're out.'
The Y2K fashion revival brought back low-rise jeans, butterfly clips, tiny sunglasses, and velour tracksuits in the early 2020s.
Bella Hadid's red Roberto Cavalli dress at the 2016 Cannes Film Festival became one of the most talked-about red carpet looks of the decade.
Chanel No. 5, introduced in 1921, was the first perfume to use synthetic aldehydes and remains one of the best-selling fragrances of all time.
The Cartier Tank watch was designed in 1917 by Louis Cartier, inspired by the angular shape of World War I Renault tanks.
Supreme's red box logo was inspired by the work of conceptual artist Barbara Kruger, leading to an ongoing debate about artistic appropriation.
The first fashion magazine, Le Mercure Galant, was published in France in 1672.
Disco fashion in the 1970s was defined by sequins, platform shoes, halter tops, and wrap dresses, with Diane von Furstenberg's wrap dress becoming iconic.
Christian Dior was born on January 21, 1905, in Granville, Normandy, France.
The global fashion industry is worth over $1.7 trillion, making it one of the largest consumer industries in the world.
Virgil Abloh passed away on November 28, 2021, from cardiac angiosarcoma, leaving an indelible mark on fashion and design.
Cosmopolitan magazine, though now known for fashion and lifestyle, was originally a family magazine first published in 1886.
Nike's 'Just Do It' slogan, introduced in 1988, was inspired by the last words of convicted murderer Gary Gilmore: 'Let's do it.'
The Burberry plaid pattern, known as the Haymarket Check, was introduced as a lining for trench coats in the 1920s.
André Leon Talley served as editor-at-large of Vogue and was one of the most influential Black figures in fashion history.
The first New York Fashion Week held at Bryant Park was in 1993, organized by the Council of Fashion Designers of America (CFDA).
Karl Lagerfeld served as creative director of Chanel for 36 years, from 1983 until his death in 2019.
Sneaker culture traces its mainstream roots to Run-DMC's 1986 hit 'My Adidas,' which led to the first hip-hop endorsement deal with a sportswear brand.
The Oscars red carpet was first rolled out in 1961 at the 33rd Academy Awards, and red carpet fashion coverage has since become a cultural event of its own.
Dior's 'New Look' used up to 20 yards of fabric per dress at a time when textiles were still rationed post-WWII, causing controversy.
W Magazine was launched in 1972 as a biweekly supplement to Women's Wear Daily (WWD) before becoming an independent publication.
The world's most expensive pair of sneakers ever sold are the Nike Air Yeezy 1 prototypes worn by Kanye West at the 2008 Grammys, which sold for $1.8 million in 2021.
Stella McCartney was appointed as an Officer of the Order of the British Empire (OBE) in 2013 for her services to fashion.
The 'heroin chic' aesthetic of the 1990s, featuring gaunt models and dark under-eye circles, sparked widespread controversy about eating disorders in the fashion industry.
Louis Vuitton was born on August 4, 1821, in Anchay, France, and walked 292 miles to Paris at age 13 to apprentice as a trunk maker.
The little black dress is so essential to fashion that it's simply referred to as the 'LBD' and is considered a wardrobe must-have across cultures.
Emily in Paris debuted on Netflix in 2020 and, despite mixed reviews, significantly boosted interest in French fashion and Parisian style.
The Levi's 501 jean has been in continuous production since 1890 and remains the best-selling jean style in the company's history.
Donatella Versace has led the Versace fashion house since 1997 and is known for her bold, glamorous aesthetic and platinum blonde hair.
The miniskirt was popularized in the 1960s by British designer Mary Quant, who named it after the Mini Cooper car because both were 'fun and accessible.'
Luxury conglomerate LVMH (Louis Vuitton Moët Hennessy) owns over 75 prestigious brands, including Dior, Fendi, Givenchy, and Tiffany & Co.
The Air Jordan line has generated over $5 billion in annual revenue for Nike, despite Michael Jordan retiring from basketball in 2003.
The 1947 Dior Bar suit, with its rounded shoulders, nipped waist, and padded hips, defined the 'New Look' silhouette.
Givenchy dressed Audrey Hepburn for decades, creating her iconic looks in Breakfast at Tiffany's (1961), including the famous black dress and pearl necklace.
The sustainable fashion movement gained major traction after the Rana Plaza factory collapse in Bangladesh on April 24, 2013, which killed 1,134 garment workers.
Vogue's September issue is the most important issue of the year, traditionally the thickest and most advertising-heavy, marking the start of the fall fashion season.
Ralph Lauren's real name is Ralph Lifshitz — he legally changed it as a teenager.
The 2023 Met Gala honoring Karl Lagerfeld featured celebrities wearing looks inspired by the late designer's signature aesthetic of black suits and fingerless gloves.
Vivienne Westwood was made a Dame Commander of the Order of the British Empire in 2006 for her services to fashion.
In the 1980s, Japanese designers like Rei Kawakubo (Comme des Garçons) and Yohji Yamamoto shocked Paris with deconstructed, avant-garde fashion.
The fashion industry uses about 93 billion cubic meters of water annually — enough to fill 37 million Olympic swimming pools.
Prada's nylon backpack, introduced in 1984, redefined luxury by making industrial material high fashion, and it remains an iconic accessory.
The first Victoria's Secret Fashion Show was held in 1995 at the Plaza Hotel in New York City.
Tom Ford launched his own brand in 2005 after leaving Gucci, establishing himself as a powerhouse in both fashion and film.
The 'Birkin' effect means that Hermès intentionally limits production to maintain exclusivity, with waiting lists that can stretch for years.
Elle magazine was founded in France in 1945 and now publishes in over 40 countries, making it the world's largest fashion magazine.
Streetwear culture blurred the lines between skateboarding, hip-hop, and high fashion starting in the 1990s, creating a new fashion paradigm.
Manolo Blahnik's shoes became a cultural phenomenon through their frequent appearances on Sex and the City, where Carrie Bradshaw called them her 'great loves.'
The Fashion Institute of Technology (FIT) in New York City was founded in 1944 and has produced designers like Calvin Klein and Michael Kors.
Coco Chanel famously said, 'Before you leave the house, look in the mirror and take one thing off.'
The world's first fashion show is believed to have been held by Charles Frederick Worth in Paris in the 1860s, using live models instead of mannequins.
Gianni Versace was born on December 2, 1946, in Reggio Calabria, Italy.
The drop culture phenomenon, where brands release limited quantities at surprise intervals, was pioneered by Supreme in the early 2000s.
Princess Diana's 'revenge dress' — the off-shoulder black Christina Stambolian dress she wore in 1994 — remains one of the most iconic fashion moments in history.
Gucci's interlocking GG logo was created in the 1960s and is one of the most recognizable logos in fashion history.
The fashion industry is the second-largest polluter in the world after the oil industry, according to several environmental studies.
Balenciaga's creative director Demna Gvasalia brought ironic luxury to the forefront with items like the $2,145 'trash bag' and the $1,790 'destroyed' sneaker.
The Chanel 2.55 bag, named after its February 1955 creation date, was revolutionary for featuring a chain strap that freed women's hands.
Converse was originally a rubber shoe company founded in 1908, and the Chuck Taylor All Star didn't appear until 1917.
Alexander McQueen was born Lee Alexander McQueen on March 17, 1969, in Lewisham, London.
GOAT (Greatest Of All Time) started in 2015 as a sneaker authentication and resale platform and has expanded to include luxury apparel and accessories.
The wrap dress, designed by Diane von Furstenberg in 1974, sold over five million units in its first two years.
André Leon Talley's 2003 documentary 'The September Issue' gave audiences a behind-the-scenes look at the production of Vogue's most important publication.
Sneaker collaborations between luxury houses and sportswear brands — like Dior x Nike Air Jordan — can sell for over $10,000 on the resale market.
Yves Saint Laurent was the first designer to use Black models in a fashion show, casting them in his 1966 collection.
The Rolex Daytona 'Paul Newman,' originally priced around $200, sold at auction for $17.8 million in 2017, making it one of the most expensive watches ever sold.
H&M, Zara, and Shein are considered the three largest fast fashion retailers in the world, with Shein overtaking them in online sales.
The power suit of the 1980s was championed by designers like Giorgio Armani, whose relaxed-yet-structured blazers became a uniform for working women.
Valentino Garavani is known for his signature 'Valentino Red' — a specific shade of red he discovered during a trip to Barcelona's opera house.
The Council of Fashion Designers of America (CFDA) was founded in 1962 and is responsible for organizing New York Fashion Week.
Vivienne Westwood was born on April 8, 1941, in Glossop, Derbyshire, England.
The concept of 'haute couture' is legally protected in France — only houses that meet strict criteria set by the Chambre Syndicale can use the term.
Off-White's signature industrial belt became a streetwear icon of the 2010s, often retailing for over $200 despite being made of nylon.
The fashion documentary 'The True Cost' (2015) exposed the environmental and human costs of fast fashion, particularly in developing countries.
Coco Chanel introduced costume jewelry as a fashion accessory in the 1920s, making it acceptable for wealthy women to wear faux gems.
Björk's swan dress, worn to the 2001 Oscars and designed by Marjan Pejoski, became one of the most controversial and memed red carpet looks of all time.
Calvin Klein was born on November 19, 1942, in the Bronx, New York.
The average garment is worn only seven times before being discarded, according to studies on consumer fashion habits.
Jimmy Choo co-founded his eponymous shoe brand in 1996 with Vogue accessories editor Tamara Mellon.
The Savile Row tailoring district in London has been the center of bespoke menswear since the early 19th century.
Pharrell Williams became creative director of Louis Vuitton's menswear in 2023, succeeding the late Virgil Abloh.
The original Air Jordan 1 'Chicago' colorway is one of the most iconic sneakers ever made, repeatedly re-released to massive demand.
Marc Jacobs' grunge collection for Perry Ellis in 1992 was critically panned and got him fired, but it's now considered one of the most influential collections ever shown.
The flapper era of the 1920s saw hemlines rise above the knee for the first time in Western fashion history.
Hermès scarves have been produced since 1937, with each silk scarf taking approximately 18 months to create from design to finished product.
The fashion industry employs approximately 75 million people worldwide, with the majority in developing countries.
Miuccia Prada took over the family business in 1978 and transformed Prada from a luggage maker into one of the world's most influential fashion houses.
America's Next Top Model, created by Tyra Banks, premiered in 2003 and ran for 24 cycles, inspiring versions in over 40 countries.
Cristóbal Balenciaga was born on January 21, 1895, in Getaria, Spain, and opened his first boutique at age 15.
The punk fashion movement of the 1970s featured safety pins, ripped clothing, leather jackets, and DIY aesthetics as a rebellion against mainstream culture.
A single cotton T-shirt requires about 2,700 liters of water to produce — enough for one person to drink for 2.5 years.
The Chanel suit, introduced in the 1950s, featured a collarless jacket and A-line skirt in tweed that became a symbol of modern femininity.
Giorgio Armani dressed more Oscar winners and nominees than any other designer, earning him the nickname 'Hollywood's favorite designer.'
The fashion 'see now, buy now' model, where collections are available immediately after being shown on the runway, gained momentum around 2016.
Vera Wang worked at Vogue for 16 years before becoming one of the most famous bridal wear designers in the world.
The world's first department store, Le Bon Marché, opened in Paris in 1838 and revolutionized how people shopped for fashion.
Yves Saint Laurent was born on August 1, 1936, in Oran, French Algeria.
Nike's collaboration with Off-White on 'The Ten' collection in 2017 is considered one of the most influential sneaker collaborations in history.
The bustle, popular in the 1870s-1880s, was an undergarment designed to expand the fullness of the back of a woman's skirt.
Vogue's first cover (1892) featured a debutante with a daisy border, and the magazine has since published over 1,500 issues.
The fashion industry produces about 92 million tons of textile waste per year, much of which ends up in landfills or incinerators.
Lady Gaga's meat dress, designed by Franc Fernandez for the 2010 MTV Video Music Awards, is now preserved in the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame.
Gabrielle 'Coco' Chanel is the only fashion designer listed on Time magazine's 100 Most Influential People of the 20th Century.
The stiletto heel was popularized in the 1950s by designers Roger Vivier and Salvatore Ferragamo, transforming women's footwear forever.
Gucci's bamboo-handled bag, introduced in 1947, was created during post-war material shortages when traditional materials were scarce.
Issey Miyake pioneered the use of technology in fashion, developing his famous pleating technique called 'Pleats Please' in the 1990s.
The CFDA Fashion Awards, often called the 'Oscars of Fashion,' have been held annually since 1981.
Rihanna's yellow Guo Pei gown at the 2015 Met Gala, with its massive train and fur trim, became one of the most iconic Met Gala looks of all time.
Tom Ford was born on August 27, 1961, in Austin, Texas.
The zipper wasn't widely used in fashion until the 1930s — before that, buttons and hooks were the standard closures.
Comme des Garçons, founded by Rei Kawakubo in Tokyo in 1969, means 'like boys' in French.
The fashion influencer economy grew into a multi-billion-dollar industry in the 2020s, with top influencers earning millions per sponsored post.
Versace's 2000 Grammy Awards dress worn by Jennifer Lopez — the plunging green jungle-print gown — is credited with inspiring the creation of Google Images.
Thom Browne revolutionized menswear in the 2000s with his shrunken-suit aesthetic, featuring cropped trousers and tight-fitting jackets.
The bikini was introduced by French designer Louis Réard on July 5, 1946, and named after the Bikini Atoll nuclear test site.
Zara's parent company Inditex can design, produce, and deliver a new garment to stores worldwide in as little as 15 days.
The bandana was first used as a fashion accessory in the American West in the 1800s and has since been adopted by countercultural movements worldwide.
Diana Vreeland served as editor-in-chief of Vogue from 1963 to 1971 and was known for her extravagant personality and avant-garde editorial vision.
The fashion industry uses an estimated 1.5 trillion liters of water annually for dyeing textiles alone.
Rick Owens is known for his dark, avant-garde aesthetic and has been called 'the Lord of Darkness' in fashion.
The sewing machine, patented by Elias Howe in 1846, revolutionized the fashion industry by making mass production of clothing possible.
Dior's J'adore perfume, launched in 1999, became one of the top-selling fragrances in the world and has been associated with Charlize Theron's golden advertising campaigns.
The platform shoe dates back to ancient Greece but became a major fashion trend in the 1970s disco era and again in the 1990s.
Vogue Italia's 'Black Issue' in July 2008, featuring only Black models, sold out and became the best-selling issue in the magazine's history.
Ann Wintour hosts the Met Gala as chair of the Metropolitan Museum of Art's Costume Institute, a role she has held since 1995.
Alexander McQueen's skull motif became his signature and helped popularize skull imagery in mainstream fashion accessories.
The bloomer, a loose-fitting trouser worn under a skirt, was introduced in the 1850s by women's rights activist Amelia Bloomer.
Lagerfeld's first collection for Chanel in 1983 featured sharp-shouldered power suits that marked a modern reinvention of Coco Chanel's codes.
Fashion Nova, founded in 2006, became one of the fastest-growing fashion brands by leveraging Instagram and celebrity partnerships, particularly with Cardi B.
Ralph Lauren was born on October 14, 1939, in the Bronx, New York.
The concept of 'athleisure' — wearing athletic clothing as casual everyday wear — became a dominant fashion trend in the 2010s, led by brands like Lululemon.
Dolce & Gabbana was founded in 1985 by Domenico Dolce and Stefano Gabbana, who met while working at an Atelier in Milan.
The bowler hat was originally designed in 1849 by London hat-makers Thomas and William Bowler for gamekeepers on the Earl of Leicester's estate.
Elsa Schiaparelli was a rival of Coco Chanel in the 1930s and is known for her surrealist-inspired designs, collaborating with Salvador Dalí.
The global luxury fashion market surpassed $350 billion in value in 2024, with China and the United States as the two largest markets.
Naomi Campbell was born on May 22, 1970, in Streatham, London.
The average fashion show lasts only about 10-15 minutes, despite months of planning and millions of dollars in production costs.
Dapper Dan, a Harlem couturier, was famous in the 1980s for creating custom luxury streetwear using logos from Gucci, Louis Vuitton, and Fendi without authorization.
The fashion industry's carbon footprint is projected to increase by more than 50% by 2030 if current trends continue.
Hubert de Givenchy and Audrey Hepburn's professional relationship lasted over 40 years, and he designed her wardrobe both on and off screen.
Ray-Ban Wayfarers became a cultural icon after being worn by James Dean, Bob Dylan, and the cast of The Breakfast Club.
The concept of 'prêt-à-porter' (ready-to-wear) was popularized in the 1960s as an alternative to haute couture, making designer fashion accessible to a wider audience.
Michael Kors started designing clothes at age 5, redesigning his mother's wedding dress.
The Chanel No. 5 bottle design, with its clean lines and minimalist aesthetic, was inspired by the modernist art movements of the 1920s.
Fashion's 'Golden Triangle' in Paris — the area between Avenue Montaigne, Avenue George V, and Rue François 1er — houses the flagship stores of the world's top fashion brands.
The first London Fashion Week in February 1984 was held in a car park on the fringes of the city's fashion district.
Balmain, founded in 1945 by Pierre Balmain, was revitalized by creative director Olivier Rousteing, who became the youngest creative director of a major fashion house at age 25.
Supermodel Linda Evangelista famously said in 1990, 'We don't wake up for less than $10,000 a day.'
The trench coat's name comes from the trenches of World War I, where British soldiers wore the weatherproof garment.
Fast fashion giant Shein was valued at approximately $66 billion in 2023, making it one of the most valuable private companies in the fashion industry.
The Christian Louboutin red sole is trademarked in the United States and the European Union, protecting it from imitation.
Dolce & Gabbana's 'Sicilian' aesthetic draws on Italian heritage with lace, religious imagery, and theatrical designs.
Mary Quant was born on February 11, 1930, in Blackheath, London.
Haute couture garments can take between 100 and 700 hours of hand-sewing to complete.
André Courrèges and Pierre Cardin were among the first designers to incorporate futuristic, space-age aesthetics into fashion in the 1960s.
The turtleneck became a symbol of intellectualism and counterculture in the 1950s and 1960s, worn by beatniks, artists, and civil rights leaders.
Victoria Beckham transitioned from pop star to respected fashion designer, launching her eponymous label in 2008.
Polyester, invented in 1941, became the dominant fabric in fast fashion because it's cheap, durable, and versatile — but it takes over 200 years to decompose.
The fedora was originally a women's hat, named after the title character in an 1882 play by Victorien Sardou.
Anna Wintour was born on November 3, 1949, in Hampstead, London.
Sneaker bots — automated software used to buy limited-edition sneakers faster than humans — became a major issue in sneaker culture starting around 2014.
Givenchy was founded by Hubert de Givenchy in 1952, and his first collection introduced the concept of 'separates' — mix-and-match luxury pieces.
The codpiece was a prominent fashion accessory for men in the 15th and 16th centuries, designed to accentuate the groin area.
Maison Margiela, founded in 1988 by Belgian designer Martin Margiela, is known for its anonymous, deconstructionist approach — Margiela himself almost never gives interviews or appears in public.
Coach, founded in 1941 in a Manhattan loft, was originally a family-run workshop handcrafting leather goods.
The 2018 Met Gala theme 'Heavenly Bodies: Fashion and the Catholic Imagination' became the museum's most visited exhibition ever.
Jean Paul Gaultier designed Madonna's iconic cone bra for her 1990 Blond Ambition World Tour.
The fashion show format of models walking a runway in sequence was established in the early 20th century, replacing earlier salon-style presentations.
Tissueweight cashmere, originally produced in the mountains of Inner Mongolia, can cost over $1,000 per garment due to the rareness of the fiber.
Hedi Slimane redefined menswear at Dior Homme in the early 2000s with his ultra-slim silhouettes, inspiring an entire generation of skinny suits and jeans.
The Panama hat is actually from Ecuador, not Panama — it got its name because workers building the Panama Canal wore them.
Burberry was founded by Thomas Burberry in 1856 in Basingstoke, Hampshire, England.
Cardi B wore a custom Thom Browne gown to the 2023 Met Gala that reportedly took 10,000 hours to create.
The French Chambre Syndicale de la Haute Couture has regulated haute couture since 1868, setting rules about handwork, ateliers, and presentations.
Swatch watches, introduced in 1983, were designed to make Swiss watches affordable and turned timepieces into fashion accessories.
The beret became a fashion staple after being popularized by French women in the 1920s and later by figures like Che Guevara and the Black Panthers.
Levi Strauss was born on February 26, 1829, in Buttenheim, Bavaria, Germany.
Salvatore Ferragamo invented the wedge heel in 1936 using cork when sanctions against Italy limited access to steel for shoe construction.
The fashion cycle traditionally had two seasons (Spring/Summer and Fall/Winter), but many brands now produce up to 52 'micro-seasons' per year.
The Chinese luxury fashion market grew to be the second largest in the world, with Gen Z consumers driving much of the growth.
John Galliano was creative director at Dior from 1996 to 2011, known for his theatrical, story-driven runway shows and exquisite craftsmanship.
The first color photograph in Vogue appeared in the July 1, 1932, issue, taken by Edward Steichen.
The catsuit, originally designed for equestrian women in the 19th century, became a fashion statement in the 1960s and has been worn by everyone from Emma Peel to Beyoncé.
Karl Lagerfeld was born on September 10, 1933, in Hamburg, Germany.
Oscar de la Renta was the first non-French designer to head a Paris haute couture house when he joined Balmain in 1993.
The concept of a 'capsule wardrobe' — a minimal collection of versatile, timeless pieces — was popularized by Susie Faux in the 1970s and later by Donna Karan.
Fashion magazines started using photography instead of illustrations in the 1930s, fundamentally changing how fashion was communicated.
Tiffany & Co. was founded by Charles Lewis Tiffany in 1837 in New York City, and the 'Tiffany Blue' box has become one of the most recognizable brand elements in luxury.
The fashion house Fendi was founded in Rome in 1925 and is famous for its fur craftsmanship and the Baguette bag.
Naomi Campbell is known for both her runway dominance and her legendary temper — she once threw a phone at her housekeeper.
The 'capsule collection' concept, where a limited-edition line is released within a larger brand, became a key marketing strategy in the 2000s.
The saddle shoe, originally designed for outdoor activities in the 1900s, became a fashion staple in the 1950s and was later revived by Prada.
Lanvin, founded in 1889 by Jeanne Lanvin, is the oldest continuously operating French fashion house.
Guccio Gucci was born on March 26, 1881, in Florence, Italy.
The New York Fashion Week schedule typically runs for about 7-9 days in February and September.
The concept of 'normcore' — intentionally dressing in plain, unfashionable clothing — emerged as an anti-fashion trend around 2014.
Alexander McQueen's final collection, shown posthumously in March 2010, was one of the most emotionally charged fashion shows ever held.
The Ugg boot originated in Australia and was popularized in the U.S. by surfers in the 1970s before becoming a global fashion phenomenon in the early 2000s.
Per the Guinness World Records, the most expensive dress ever made was a diamond-encrusted wedding dress worth $12 million by Renee Strauss.
Isabel Marant popularized the 'effortless Parisian chic' aesthetic with her bohemian-inspired designs and signature wedge sneakers.
Circular fashion, which focuses on designing products with their next use in mind, gained momentum as a response to fashion waste.
Rei Kawakubo was born on October 11, 1942, in Tokyo, Japan.
The leopard print has been a fashion staple since the 1940s, when Christian Dior first used it in haute couture, though it was popular as far back as ancient Egypt.
The Met Gala's ticket price reached $75,000 per person and $350,000 per table by the 2020s, making it one of the most exclusive events in the world.
Pierre Cardin was one of the first designers to license his name, eventually putting it on over 800 products from pens to sardines.
The Chelsea boot was originally designed for Queen Victoria in 1851 and was later adopted by the Beatles and the mod subculture.
Louis Vuitton trunks were originally flat-topped (rather than rounded) so they could be stacked — a design innovation that helped build the brand.
Chanel's tweed fabric is produced exclusively at the Linton Tweeds mill in Carlisle, England, which has supplied the fashion house since the 1920s.
The first fashion blog, 'The Sartorialist' by Scott Schuman, launched in 2005 and helped legitimize street style as its own fashion category.
Valentino retired from fashion in 2008 after his final haute couture show in Paris, ending a career that spanned nearly 50 years.
Synthetic fabrics like polyester, nylon, and acrylic make up about 65% of all textiles produced globally.
The biker jacket, first designed by Irving Schott in 1928 (the Perfecto), became an icon of rebellion after Marlon Brando wore one in The Wild One (1953).
Hermès's annual revenue exceeded $14 billion in 2023, driven largely by its leather goods and accessories divisions.
Anna Wintour's signature sunglasses are by Chanel — she once said she wears them so people can't see who she's looking at during fashion shows.
Fashion rental services like Rent the Runway, founded in 2009, popularized the idea that fashion could be accessed rather than owned.
The fashion documentary 'McQueen' (2018) chronicled Alexander McQueen's rise from London's East End to the pinnacle of fashion before his death in 2010.
The hobble skirt, popular around 1910, was so narrow at the hem that women could barely walk, sparking public debate about fashion and freedom.
The Nike Dunk, originally a basketball shoe from 1985, evolved into one of the most popular lifestyle sneakers, particularly through its 'SB' (Skateboarding) line.
The 2022 Met Gala theme was 'In America: An Anthology of Fashion,' and the dress code was 'Gilded Glamour' in honor of the Gilded Age.
Haute couture requires a minimum of 15 full-time staff and at least 20 skilled artisans (petites mains) working in the atelier.
Kate Moss's 'nothing tastes as good as skinny feels' quote in 2009 sparked intense backlash and reignited debates about body image in fashion.
The fashion industry is increasingly using AI for trend forecasting, virtual try-ons, and even designing garments.
Yves Saint Laurent's 'Mondrian' collection (1965) featured shift dresses inspired by the geometric paintings of Piet Mondrian.
The kaftan, originally from the Ottoman Empire, became a bohemian fashion staple in the 1960s and 70s.
Virgil Abloh was born on September 30, 1980, in Rockford, Illinois.
The most expensive handbag ever sold at auction is a Hermès Himalaya Birkin with diamond hardware, which went for over $500,000.
Fashion photographer Helmut Newton was known for his provocative, high-contrast black-and-white images that blurred the line between art and fashion.
The invention of the cotton gin by Eli Whitney in 1793 revolutionized the textile industry and made cotton clothing widely affordable.
Diesel's 'For Successful Living' advertising campaigns of the 1990s were among the most provocative and ironic in fashion history.
The Adidas Superstar, introduced in 1969 as a basketball shoe, became a hip-hop icon after Run-DMC adopted it without laces in the 1980s.
Marc Jacobs became the creative director of Louis Vuitton in 1997 and served until 2014, transforming the luggage house into a fashion powerhouse.
Coco Chanel closed her fashion house during World War II and reopened in 1954 at age 71, staging a comeback that many initially criticized but ultimately cemented her legacy.
The jelly shoe, made from PVC plastic, was invented in the early 1980s and became a symbol of affordable, playful fashion.
Paris Fashion Week typically runs for about nine days in late September/early October and late February/early March.
The Cartier Love bracelet, designed in 1969, requires a special screwdriver to put on and take off, symbolizing an unbreakable bond.
The fashion industry's reliance on social media marketing exploded in the 2010s, with Instagram becoming the primary platform for fashion discovery.
Zac Posen was designing dresses for his classmates at age 16 and showed his first collection at age 21.
Twiggy, born Lesley Lawson, became the face of 1960s fashion at age 16, known for her androgynous figure, short hair, and painted lower lashes.
The espadrille, a rope-soled shoe originating from the Pyrenees region, has been worn since at least the 14th century.
The fashion term 'prêt-à-porter' literally means 'ready to wear' in French, distinguishing mass-produced clothing from one-of-a-kind haute couture.
Maison Margiela's 'Tabi' boot, featuring a split toe inspired by Japanese tabi socks, has been a cult fashion item since 1988.
Diane von Furstenberg was born on December 31, 1946, in Brussels, Belgium.
The global secondhand apparel market is expected to reach $350 billion by 2027, driven by sustainability concerns and platforms like Depop, Poshmark, and ThredUp.
Grace Kelly's wedding dress, designed by Helen Rose of MGM Studios in 1956, used 25 yards of silk taffeta, 100 yards of silk net, and antique rose-point lace.
The pearl necklace has been a fashion staple since ancient Rome and was modernized by Coco Chanel, who layered multiple strands for a casual-elegant look.
Fashion weeks generate an estimated $600 million in economic activity for New York City alone each season.
The Birkenstock sandal, created in 1774, went from unfashionable 'hippie shoe' to high fashion after collaborations with designers like Rick Owens and Valentino.
Thierry Mugler's fall 1995 couture show, featuring insect-inspired designs and cyborg-like costumes, is considered one of the most spectacular fashion shows ever held.
New Balance sneakers became a luxury fashion item in the 2020s through collaborations with designers like Joe Freshgoods and Aimé Leon Dore.
The fashion term 'décolletage' — exposed neckline and cleavage — comes from the French verb 'décolleter,' meaning 'to reveal the neck.'
Donna Karan's '7 Easy Pieces' collection in 1985 introduced a practical approach to women's workwear that could be mixed and matched.
The paisley pattern, originally from Persia, became associated with Western fashion after shawls featuring the design were imported to Europe in the 18th century.
Vivienne Westwood passed away on December 29, 2022, at the age of 81, leaving behind a legacy of punk, activism, and boundary-pushing fashion.
The first successful commercial synthetic dye, mauveine, was discovered accidentally by William Henry Perkin in 1856 and transformed the fashion color palette.
Jacquemus, founded by Simon Porte Jacquemus in 2009 at age 19, became known for its tiny 'Le Chiquito' bag that went viral on social media.
The average American buys approximately 68 garments per year, a figure that has doubled since the 1990s.
The Met Gala was first organized by publicist Eleanor Lambert in 1948 as a midnight supper fundraiser for the Costume Institute.
The trench coat's design features — D-rings, storm flaps, and epaulettes — all had practical military origins before becoming fashion details.
Carhartt, founded in 1889 as workwear, was adopted by hip-hop culture in the 1990s and later spawned its own fashion-forward line, Carhartt WIP.
The clutch bag, a small handheld purse without a strap, became a popular fashion accessory in the 1920s-1930s.
The idea of seasonal fashion collections dates back to Charles Frederick Worth in the mid-1800s, who is considered the father of haute couture.
Versace's Medusa head logo was chosen from Greek mythology — Gianni said he was drawn to Medusa because she symbolized the power of beauty.
Balenciaga Triple S sneakers, launched in 2017, helped launch the 'ugly shoe' trend in luxury fashion.
The silk road, active from about 130 BC to 1453 AD, was the primary conduit for silk and textile trade between Asia and Europe, shaping fashion history for centuries.
Marchesa, known for red carpet gowns, was founded in 2004 by Georgina Chapman and Keren Craig, named after socialite Marchesa Luisa Casati.
The go-go boot, introduced by André Courrèges in 1964, became a symbol of the swinging sixties and women's liberation.
Gabrielle Chanel died on January 10, 1971, at the Ritz Paris hotel, where she had lived for more than 30 years.
Over 60% of fashion brands failed to disclose information about their supply chains in 2023, according to the Fashion Transparency Index.
Phoebe Philo's minimalist tenure at Céline (2008-2018) defined a generation of understated luxury and inspired the 'quiet luxury' trend.
The pearl has been called 'the queen of gems' and was so valued in ancient Rome that Julius Caesar passed a law limiting pearl-wearing to the ruling classes.
Tommy Hilfiger became a major figure in the intersection of fashion and hip-hop in the 1990s, dressing artists like Aaliyah, TLC, and Snoop Dogg.
The largest fashion collection ever shown on a runway was by Chanel's Karl Lagerfeld, whose spring 2015 show featured a fake protest march with 79 looks.
Dolce & Gabbana's Alta Moda (high fashion) shows are invitation-only events held in iconic locations like Capri, Venice, and the Valley of the Temples in Sicily.
Kim Kardashian's 2021 Met Gala Balenciaga full-body covering by Demna sparked massive debate about the nature of fashion and celebrity.
Charles Frederick Worth, born on October 13, 1825, is widely considered the 'father of haute couture.'
The average pair of jeans travels about 20,000 miles during the manufacturing process — from cotton farm to dye house to factory to store.
Mugler's Alien perfume bottle was inspired by a precious stone and has become one of the most recognizable fragrance vessels in the world.
A/W and S/S stand for Autumn/Winter and Spring/Summer, the two primary seasons around which the fashion calendar is organized.
The first fashion show live-streamed on the internet was Alexander McQueen's Spring 2010 show, 'Plato's Atlantis.'
The durag, originally used to maintain hairstyles, became a fashion accessory in its own right after appearing on runways and in hip-hop culture.
Rick Owens was born on November 18, 1962, in Porterville, California.
Fashion houses like Dior and Chanel destroy unsold merchandise to maintain brand exclusivity, though this practice has faced increasing scrutiny.
Batik, a technique of wax-resist dyeing on fabric, originated in Indonesia and has influenced fashion designers worldwide.
The sari, worn for over 5,000 years in South Asia, is one of the oldest and most continuously worn garments in fashion history.
Raf Simons' tenure at Calvin Klein (2016-2018) was critically acclaimed but commercially unsuccessful, highlighting the tension between art and commerce in fashion.
The fashion industry's size inclusivity movement gained significant momentum in the 2020s, with more brands offering extended size ranges.
The Adidas Stan Smith, originally named the Adidas Robert Haillet in 1965, was renamed in 1978 and became one of the best-selling sneakers of all time.
Salvatore Ferragamo invented the cage heel and the invisible sandal using nylon fishing line, earning him patents and the nickname 'shoemaker of dreams.'
Hubert de Givenchy was born on February 21, 1927, in Beauvais, France.
The 'investment piece' concept — buying fewer, higher-quality items that last longer — has become central to the anti-fast-fashion movement.
The color Valentino Red has its own Pantone code (number 19-1663 TPX) and is the brand's most recognizable signature.
Fashion illustration was the primary way to communicate designs before photography took over in the 1930s, with artists like René Gruau creating iconic images for Dior.
The poncho, originating from indigenous South American cultures, became a bohemian fashion staple in the 1960s and has periodically returned to the runway.
Vera Wang was a competitive figure skater before becoming a fashion journalist at Vogue and eventually a world-renowned bridal designer.
The global counterfeit fashion market is estimated at over $500 billion annually, with luxury handbags being among the most frequently faked items.
Elsa Schiaparelli's 'Lobster Dress' (1937), created in collaboration with Salvador Dalí, is one of the most famous examples of surrealism in fashion.
The necktie evolved from Croatian mercenaries' neck scarves worn during the Thirty Years' War in the 17th century — the French called them 'cravates.'
Fashion month, encompassing all four major fashion weeks, lasts approximately one month from early September to early October each fall season.
Brandon Maxwell, Lady Gaga's stylist-turned-designer, launched his own label in 2015 and became creative director of Walmart's fashion brands in 2023.
The kilt, traditionally associated with Scottish culture, has appeared in high fashion collections from Jean Paul Gaultier, Vivienne Westwood, and Thom Browne.
Miuccia Prada holds a PhD in political science from the University of Milan and was a member of Italy's Communist Party before entering fashion.
The first ready-to-wear collection shown during Paris Haute Couture Week was by Yves Saint Laurent in 1966, democratizing fashion.
Cartier, founded in Paris in 1847, is known as 'the jeweler of kings and the king of jewelers,' a title given by King Edward VII.
The crop top dates back to South Asian fashion traditions thousands of years old, though it entered Western mainstream fashion in the 1940s.
Fashion designer Halston defined 1970s American glamour with his minimalist, fluid designs worn by Bianca Jagger, Liza Minnelli, and Jackie Kennedy.
Anna Sui opened her first boutique in 1992 in New York's SoHo neighborhood, known for her rock-and-roll-inspired designs.
The most-liked Instagram fashion post of all time has garnered hundreds of millions of interactions, demonstrating social media's dominance in fashion marketing.
Pierre Cardin was born on July 2, 1922, in San Biagio di Callalta, Italy.
The first synthetic fabric, nylon, was introduced by DuPont in 1938 and revolutionized the hosiery industry almost overnight.
Demna Gvasalia's appointment as creative director of Balenciaga in 2015 marked a new era of ironic, meme-driven luxury fashion.
The bucket hat, originally designed for Irish fishermen, became a streetwear staple in the 1990s hip-hop scene and returned in the 2020s.
Jil Sander, known as the 'Queen of Less,' pioneered minimalist fashion in the 1970s with clean lines and luxurious fabrics.
Fashion photographer Richard Avedon transformed the medium by capturing models in motion on the streets of Paris rather than in static studio poses.
The peplum silhouette — a short overskirt or ruffle at the waist — dates back to ancient Greek peplos garments and resurges periodically in contemporary fashion.
Jonathan Anderson became creative director of both his own brand JW Anderson and Spanish house Loewe, making him one of fashion's busiest designers.
The 'quiet luxury' trend of 2023, featuring logo-free, understated clothing from brands like Loro Piana and Brunello Cucinelli, was fueled by the show Succession.
Miu Miu, Prada's sister brand founded in 1993, is named after Miuccia Prada's nickname and targets a younger, edgier audience.
The world's oldest known leather shoe, dating back over 5,500 years, was discovered in a cave in Armenia in 2008.
